lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing hourly, daily, or monthly vehicle parking, valet parking, and outsourced parking-facility operations to motorists or property owners, including management-contract and leased-facility models.
lens.excluded_activities: Extended vehicle storage classified in 493190, property owners that only lease real estate without operating parking services, captive internal parking departments, software-only parking platforms without accountable operations, municipal departments, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators collect transient or monthly parking fees from motorists and/or fixed, variable, performance, technology, and ancillary-service fees from property owners. Work spans staffing, valet, customer assistance, payment and revenue control, enforcement, facility upkeep, shuttle operations, marketing, and technology-enabled access or reservations.

a.rationale: Current not-yet-automated exposure is concentrated in customer support, exception handling, monitoring, revenue reconciliation, enforcement review, staffing, marketing, forecasting, and administrative work. Mobile payment, prebooking, remote monitoring, automated equipment, and digital revenue management already remove some routine tasks, while valet driving, shuttle driving, patrol, cleaning, repairs, and on-site incident response remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary across cashiers, attendants, valets, shuttle drivers, enforcement staff, maintenance staff, remote support, location managers, and back-office teams at independent 812930 operators, with existing automation identified, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: No six-digit task-exposure study was found; SP Plus was a large diversified operator with aviation and technology operations beyond the LMM parking lens.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible because mobile commerce, remote 24-hour support, parking enforcement technology, valet tracking, and revenue-management systems are already deployed. Fragmented site hardware, integration with property owners, safety, labor rules, payment security, municipal requirements, and the need for reliable on-site escalation constrain adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor hours, service levels, loss rates, occupancy, and incident outcomes from AI deployments at at least twenty parking facilities across management, lease, valet, and municipal-enforcement models would establish realized implementation.
caveats: The source demonstrates technical and operational feasibility but does not measure AI adoption or realized labor substitution at LMM operators.

e.rationale: Census defines the code around establishments selling parking space or valet services, and the public-company evidence confirms recurring outsourced management contracts are a core model. Most firms in the supplied LMM band should therefore be eligible, with a discount for captive operations, property-owning entities, software vendors, nonrecurring event-only providers, and classification error.
e.change_evidence: A business-register tabulation linking 812930 firms to external parking-service revenue, management or lease contracts, independence, and normalized EBITDA would determine the eligible share.
caveats: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and NAICS primary activity does not establish independence, contract recurrence, or the exact EBITDA band.

s5.rationale: Recurring contracts, local density, operating systems, and property-owner relationships can make parking operators transferable, and the cited filing describes a fragmented market in which larger providers may acquire smaller operators. Control transfers remain constrained by contract termination rights, client concentration, low differentiation, labor liabilities, and the risk that key site contracts do not survive a sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year PitchBook, GF Data, DealStats, or BizBuySell extract of verified 812930 control sales, matched to eligible firms and contract-retention outcomes, would replace this judgment.
caveats: Industry fragmentation and one large-company acquisition do not establish an LMM annual transfer rate; purchases of isolated contracts are not necessarily qualifying control sales.

q.rationale: Lease-type facilities can retain more operating benefit until rent resets, but management contracts dominate the representative source and typically pay fixed or variable fees while clients bear many operating expenses. One-to-three-year management terms, open-book cost reimbursement, competitive rebids, and technology-fee negotiation create regular pass-through; convenience fees and performance incentives preserve some upside.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level fee, reimbursement, renewal, labor-budget, technology-charge, and margin data before and after automation across management and lease facilities would reveal the discounted share retained by operators.
caveats: SP Plus reported 88% of commercial locations under management contracts, but the revenue and contract mix of independent LMM firms may differ materially.

d5.rationale: Parking remains tied to vehicle trips and destinations across offices, residences, hospitals, hotels, retail, events, universities, and airports. Hybrid work, transit, ride-hailing, autonomous mobility, curb reallocation, and real-estate redevelopment can reduce particular locations, while population, travel, events, congestion management, prebooking, and professional outsourcing support demand. Without a representative constant-quality volume series, a near-flat base and broad range are judgment.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of inflation-adjusted parked-vehicle transactions, monthly permits, occupied stall-hours, and management-contract volumes across facility types and metros would materially refine the ratio.
caveats: The cited 94% commercial-facility retention rate measures client retention at one large operator, not constant-quality parking demand or future vehicle volume.

o.rationale: Reservation, access, payment, basic monitoring, and routine help can become self-service or centrally remote. An accountable operator remains necessary for property-owner contracting, pricing and revenue control, enforcement policy, equipment uptime, safety, incident response, facility condition, and services such as valet, shuttle, cleaning, repairs, and special-event operations.
o.change_evidence: Facility-level data separating fully unattended transactions from remote and on-site labor, together with responsibility for incidents, enforcement, maintenance, and customer recovery, would establish the operator-required share.
caveats: Software platforms and property owners may internalize parts of the workflow, while valet, shuttle, and maintenance-heavy locations require much more operator labor than automated garages.

narrative.executive_view: Parking operations combine a high compensation burden with proven mobile, remote-monitoring, revenue-management, and enforcement technology, creating meaningful workflow opportunity. The service remains site-specific and operationally accountable, while management-contract economics, rebidding, and cost reimbursement limit how much benefit an operator retains.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can handle routine customer contacts, detect payment or enforcement exceptions, forecast occupancy, support dynamic pricing, reconcile revenue, optimize staffing and shuttle schedules, and monitor equipment. It cannot eliminate valet and shuttle driving, patrol, cleaning, repairs, safety response, client accountability, or site-specific judgment.
narrative.value_capture: Lease operators may retain gains until rent or pricing resets, but outsourced management clients often fund payroll and operating costs and negotiate fees on short contracts. Performance fees, technology fees, convenience fees, and better utilization provide capture paths, though competitive renewal pressure is strong.
narrative.firm_availability: The code is centered on operating lots, garages, and valet services, and a fragmented market supports a population of independent operators. Transfer quality depends on durable client contracts and local density; the supplied LMM count is still only a margin-bridged estimate.
narrative.demand_durability: Parking demand is diversified across many destinations but location-specific. Hybrid work and mobility substitution weaken some office facilities, while hospitals, residential properties, travel, events, and outsourced municipal or institutional operations are more durable.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are missing six-digit task and transfer data, a large-operator source outside the LMM band, client contract termination or rebid, wage and insurance pressure, payment and privacy regulation, uneven site hardware, and long-run changes in commuting, land use, and vehicle ownership.
narrative.principal_driver: Digital payment, remote support, enforcement, pricing, and scheduling can improve a labor-intensive recurring operation with measurable client relationships.
narrative.principal_weakness: Management-contract pass-through and site-level physical obligations constrain retained economics even when automation is feasible.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 812930 Parking Lots and Garages
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments providing parking space hourly, daily, or monthly and/or valet parking services, while excluding extended vehicle storage.

sources.title: SP Plus Corporation 2023 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes management and lease contracts, personnel and revenue-control responsibilities, mobile and online parking, remote 24-hour support, enforcement, valet, shuttle and maintenance services; reports typical management terms of 1-3 years, lease terms of 3-10 years, 88% of commercial locations under management contracts, and 94% commercial-facility retention in 2023.
